channel.title: State-Policy Think-Tank Digest
channel.description: Latest posts from free-market policy institutes across the U.S.

item.title: [Foropportunity] GCO Praises Federal Leaders for Empowering States to Strengthen Pathways to Work through the Stronger Workforce for America Act
item.description:
The Stronger Workforce for America Act of 2026 includes an invaluable option for state flexibility, the Make America Skilled Again Grants. Georgia and other states need this option so they can design workforce and safety net systems that meet families' immediate needs while providing a clearer path to opportunity and self-sufficiency.

The post GCO Praises Federal Leaders for Empowering States to Strengthen Pathways to Work through the Stronger Workforce for America Act appeared first on Georgia Center For Opportunity.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Nebraskanomics: William Beard on how Nebraska Competes for Data Centers
item.description:
Tune in to the latest episode of Nebraskanomics as we welcome William Beard, co-author of the Goldwater Institute’s report: Data Centers a Free Market Model for the Digital Future. During the episode William and Platte CEO Jim Vokal discuss what data centers are, why they matter, and what separates the states that are winning investment...

The post Nebraskanomics: William Beard on how Nebraska Competes for Data Centers appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Maine’s Law Court Gets it Right: RCV Violates Maine Constitution
item.description:
The Maine Supreme Judicial Court has issued a unanimous advisory opinion in which the Court concluded that the Legislature’s attempt to expand ranked-choice voting (RCV) to general elections for governor, state senator, and state representative would violate the Maine Constitution.  Although the decision came quickly, the outcome closely tracks the concerns that surfaced during oral […]

The post Maine’s Law Court Gets it Right: RCV Violates Maine Constitution appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] A Step in the Right Direction on Energy, Now Let’s Finish the Job
item.description:
by Wells Winegar New Jersey just made a long overdue move. With Governor Mikie Sherrill signing legislation to lift the […]

The post A Step in the Right Direction on Energy, Now Let’s Finish the Job first appeared on New Jersey Policy Institute.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Idaho ends taxpayer support for teachers’ unions
item.description:
Idaho Gov. Brad Little on April 10 signed into law a Freedom Foundation-supported measure crafted to end taxpayer support for teachers' unions in the Gem State. 

The post Idaho ends taxpayer support for teachers’ unions appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Empowerms] U.S. Senator Roger Wicker: Strength Abroad, Opportunity at Home
item.description:
U.S. Senator Roger Wicker: Strength Abroad, Opportunity at Home In a time of growing global instability, the role of American strength is back at the center of the conversation. In this episode, Grant sits down with Roger Wicker, Chairman of the Senate Armed Services Committee and Mississippi’s senior senator. From rebuilding America’s military strength to…

The post U.S. Senator Roger Wicker: Strength Abroad, Opportunity at Home first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] Why New Jersey’s Small Businesses Deserve Better Policy
item.description:
by Megan Whitman, NJPI Jr. Fellow Walk down any main street in New Jersey and you’re looking at the heart […]

The post Why New Jersey’s Small Businesses Deserve Better Policy first appeared on New Jersey Policy Institute.

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] Alabama lawmakers deliver 29 of 30 conservative victories from API’s ‘record-breaking’ 2026 agenda
item.description:
The Alabama Policy Institute on Monday declared the 2026 regular session its most productive on record.

The post Alabama lawmakers deliver 29 of 30 conservative victories from API’s ‘record-breaking’ 2026 agenda appeared first on Alabama Policy Institute.

item.title: [Rifreedom] Law Centre Calls on Rhode Island Department of Health to Stop Enforcing Conversion Therapy Ban After Supreme Court Ruling
item.description:
The Law Centre today delivered a formal letter to Dr. Jerome Larkin, Director of the Rhode Island Department of Health (RIDOH), demanding that the agency immediately cease enforcement of Rhode Island’s ban on conversion therapy for minors. The action follows the U.S. Supreme Court’s March 21, 2026 decision in Chiles v. Salazar, which struck down Colorado’s similar law as a violation of the First Amendment.

The post Law Centre Calls on Rhode Island Department of Health to Stop Enforcing Conversion Therapy Ban After Supreme Court Ruling appeared first on RI Center for Freedom and Prosperity.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] 2026 Green Book
item.description: 2026 Kansas Green Book – Exploring the relationship between the size of government and economic growth

item.title: [Freedomfoundationofminnesota] Minnesota Parents, We’re About to Lose Out on $800 Million for Our Children
item.description:
By Zach Freimark and Sarah Hitchcock America First Policy Institute On July 4, 2025, Congress passed, on a bipartisan vote, a package of reconciliations, commonly referred to as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. Included in the bill was a provision that created the Education Freedom Tax Credit (EFTC), which funds elementary and secondary education […]

The post Minnesota Parents, We’re About to Lose Out on $800 Million for Our Children appeared first on Freedom Foundation of Minnesota.

item.title: [Empowerms] 2026 Capitol Chat: Sine Die
item.description:
Capitol Chat: Sine Die Lawmakers returned to Jackson expecting veto overrides, but what followed was one of the more unusual endings to a legislative session in recent memory. We unpack the final day’s twists, the political fallout, and close the book on the 2026 session.

The post 2026 Capitol Chat: Sine Die first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Freedomfoundationofminnesota] “Tax Me More” Fund Not Seeing Much Action Despite Tax Hikers’ False Appeals to Patriotism
item.description:
Americans are allowed to voluntarily contribute money to the U.S. Treasury’s “tax me more” fund. But not much money is being donated despite the false appeals to “patriotism” peddled by tax hikers. In calling for tax increases, affluent Democrats and progressive activists try to assert that Americans opposed to such tax hikes are lacking in […]

The post “Tax Me More” Fund Not Seeing Much Action Despite Tax Hikers’ False Appeals to Patriotism appeared first on Freedom Foundation of Minnesota.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] Secretary of State Bellows Butchers Another Ballot Question
item.description:
In the fall, Maine voters will have the opportunity to vote on a citizen referendum aimed at protecting girls’ sports by requiring schools to separate athletic teams and certain facilities based on biological sex as recorded at birth. Before they ever get the chance to vote on the policy, however, there is a critically important […]

The post Secretary of State Bellows Butchers Another Ballot Question appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Kansas Has a Cost Problem—And It’s Driving People Away
item.description: Kansas isn’t collapsing. That would at least get attention. What’s happening is quieter and more dangerous: the state is becoming too expensive, too fragmented, and too average to compete, while other states pull ahead. The new 2026 Kansas Green Book lays it out clearly. Kansas is not leading where it matters. It is lagging, and […]

item.title: [Freedomfoundationofminnesota] Why Walz’s proposed social media tax is a bad idea
item.description:
It would run afoul of the First Amendment, among other issues. By Annette Meeks Guest contributor to the Minnesota Star Tribune April 20, 2026 ••• What do progressives do when they run out of other people’s money to spend? You don’t have to look any further than Minnesota to find out: Recently Gov. Tim Walz […]

The post Why Walz’s proposed social media tax is a bad idea appeared first on Freedom Foundation of Minnesota.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] What Unions Were Meant to Be — And What They’ve Become, A Story of Self Destruction
item.description:
Unions were created with a simple mission to perform: Protecting workers. Standing in between employees and abuse.

The post What Unions Were Meant to Be — And What They’ve Become, A Story of Self Destruction appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] The Measure of a Man
item.description: House Bill 461, introduced late in the 2025 legislative session, would have required the state to run an analysis of the Idaho Launch program to determine its effectiveness. I won’t bore you with all the details, but essentially the question is: “Does this program help the state the way Gov. Brad Little and other proponents […]

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] President Trump, Speaker Johnson, National Leaders, API Board & Staff to read passages at America Reads the Bible Event in DC
item.description:
For 84 hours over 8 days almost 500 national leaders will read the entire bible from start to finish.

The post President Trump, Speaker Johnson, National Leaders, API Board & Staff to read passages at America Reads the Bible Event in DC appeared first on Alabama Policy Institute.

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] Oregon Should Opt Into the Federal Scholarship Tax Credit So Private Giving Stays with Oregon Kids
item.description:
By Kathryn Hickok Last summer, Congress passed the first federal-level legislation promoting educational...

The post Oregon Should Opt Into the Federal Scholarship Tax Credit So Private Giving Stays with Oregon Kids appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Foropportunity] Strengthening Her Family, Building Her Community: Shaneika Murphy’s Passion for Helping Others Flourish
item.description:
After completing the Raising Highly Capable Kids workshop as a parent, Shaneika Murphy now supports other parents as a program facilitator. Her sons volunteer with the program, too. Together, the Murphys are a shining example of how strong families build strong communities.

The post Strengthening Her Family, Building Her Community: Shaneika Murphy’s Passion for Helping Others Flourish appeared first on Georgia Center For Opportunity.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Easy graders make real life harder
item.description:
Students learn less and earn less when they have less incentive to actually study in order to get top grades.

The post Easy graders make real life harder appeared first on Badger Institute.

item.title: [Grassrootinstitute] Insights from rebuilding Lahaina could inform Kona low response
item.description:
Many of the lessons learned from Maui County’s ongoing wildfire recovery efforts could help streamline statewide efforts to rebuild flood-damaged homes, farms, businesses and infrastructure following the recent Kona low storms, according to Jonathan Helton, policy analyst at the Grassroot Institute of Hawaii. Helton detailed some of those lessons in an April 12 radio interview […]

The post Insights from rebuilding Lahaina could inform Kona low response appeared first on Grassroot Institute of Hawaii.

item.title: [Grassrootinstitute] Tax relief needed to help combat Hawaii’s high cost of living
item.description:
The following “Island Voices” commentary was first published April 22, 2026, in the Honolulu Star-Advertiser under the headline “Now is no time to retreat on proposed isle tax cuts.”_____________ Less than two years ago, Hawaii lawmakers unanimously passed historic income tax cuts to help relieve residents from one of the highest tax burdens in the country. Now, […]

The post Tax relief needed to help combat Hawaii’s high cost of living appeared first on Grassroot Institute of Hawaii.

item.title: [Jamesmadison] Better Broadband Needs Better Permitting Rules
item.description:
In rural Utah, a broadband provider waited nearly three years for permission to repair an existing cable line...

The post Better Broadband Needs Better Permitting Rules appeared first on James Madison Institute.

item.title: [Freedomfoundation] Double standards in Pueblo 70: Union power, taxpayer dollars and selective outrage
item.description:
After weeks of intense public pressure, including loud demands from union supporters, the Pueblo County School District 70 board approved a generous extension of its collective bargaining agreement with the Pueblo County Education Association.

The post Double standards in Pueblo 70: Union power, taxpayer dollars and selective outrage appeared first on Freedom Foundation.

item.title: [Jbartlett] US public school spending hits $1 trillion; NH ranks 7th per-pupil
item.description:
Spending on K-12 public education in the United States passed the $1 trillion mark in the 2024 fiscal year, just-released data from the federal National Center for Education Statistics (NCES) reveal. “Time to update all mentions of the size of this sector,” commented the Edunomics Lab at Georgetown University’s McCourt School of Public Policy. “Revenues […]

The post US public school spending hits $1 trillion; NH ranks 7th per-pupil appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Beacontn] From Atoms to Algorithms: Positioning Tennessee to be America’s Energy Leader
item.description: Tennessee stands at a critical juncture facing unprecedented demands on the state’s energy infrastructure. By leveraging key opportunities, Tennessee can not only meet its own energy needs but also cement its role as a national model for abundant energy generation.

item.title: [Beacontn] Positioning Tennessee to Be America’s Energy Leader
item.description: Tennessee’s rich nuclear history shows the state is a leader in nuclear energy and advanced technologies. With new technology and rising electricity demand, there are now opportunities for Tennessee to not only meet the energy needs of its growing population but also to be a model for the whole country in developing and deploying affordable, abundant energy.

item.title: [Grassrootinstitute] The unintended consequences of transfer taxes
item.description:
Most of the attention this year at the state Legislature has been focused on the status of our historic income tax cuts from 2024 — whether they are going to be modified or repealed, effectively resulting in a tax hike. But there’s another tax-hike bill still alive that has been quietly making its way through […]

The post The unintended consequences of transfer taxes appeared first on Grassroot Institute of Hawaii.

item.title: [Empirecenter] New York’s Electricity Prices 70 Percent Above the National Average
item.description:
Recent data from the Energy Information Administration and Empire Center for Public Policy show New York’s average residential electricity prices....

The post New York’s Electricity Prices 70 Percent Above the National Average appeared first on Empire Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Foropportunity] Raising Highly Capable Kids Program Increases Positive Values and Social Skills in Children
item.description:
GCO and its partners offered 15 RHCK workshops at 12 locations in 2025. Participants took pre- and post-workshop surveys that illustrated the ways families had grown stronger throughout the program.

The post Raising Highly Capable Kids Program Increases Positive Values and Social Skills in Children appeared first on Georgia Center For Opportunity.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Inaugural US DataLabs Ranking Finds Alaska the State Most Dependent on Federal Funds, Idaho the Least
item.description:
Per-capita analysis reveals surprising exposure in Virginia and Massachusetts, offering a fuller picture of how deeply federal dollars shape state economies 

The post Inaugural US DataLabs Ranking Finds Alaska the State Most Dependent on Federal Funds, Idaho the Least appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Empowerms] Michael Watson: Faith, Family, and Public Service
item.description:
Michael Watson: Faith, Family, and Public Service Mississippi Secretary of State Michael Watson returns to the Empower Podcast as he launches his campaign for Lt. Governor. In this conversation, Grant sits down with Watson for a more personal look at the experiences and convictions that have shaped his leadership. From growing up on the Gulf…

The post Michael Watson: Faith, Family, and Public Service first appeared on Empower Mississippi.

item.title: [Newjerseypolicyinstitute] The NJEA Is Putting Politics Before Kids, Again
item.description:
New Jersey should opt into the Education Freedom Tax Credit. The union’s opposition tells you everything about whose interests it […]

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Buried Without a Vote: The Secret Power of Committee Chairmen
item.description: It isn’t a power afforded in House or Senate rules. It isn’t written into Mason’s Manual of Legislative Procedure, and it certainly isn’t penned into the Constitution. Yet, during legislative sessions at the Idaho Capitol, it is utilized and treated as an authorized exercise of power.  We are, of course, talking about the chairman’s drawer. […]

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] New Study Finds Massachusetts Business Formation Has Plummeted Despite National Surge
item.description:
The Commonwealth had the nation’s lowest net business formation rate from 2020 to 2024, losing more than 17,000 employer businesses over nine consecutive quarters 

The post New Study Finds Massachusetts Business Formation Has Plummeted Despite National Surge appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Inpolicy] Eichenberger: Pride — From Icarus to Instagram
item.description: by Dan Eichenberger MD, MBA Ancient civilizations recognized pride, or hubris, as a recurring human vulnerability with serious consequences. In Greek mythology and tragedy, excessive arrogance — defying natural limits or overestimating one’s place—invariably led to nemesis, or downfall. The tale of Icarus, who ignored his father’s warnings and flew too close to the sun, melting Read the full article...

item.title: [Inpolicy] The Outstater
item.description: Let’s Pack the U.S. House GALLUP REPORTS this week that the approval rate of Congress has fallen to 10 percent. Coincidentally, a Butler graduate student, Evan Wareham, has an intriguing idea — reapportionment on steroids. What if we reformed the U.S. House of Representatives by adding members to increase per-capita representation? That, please know, was the original plan. In Read the full article...

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Nebraskanomics: ALEC President Jonathan Williams on Nebraska’s Latest Ranking
item.description:
Tune in to the latest episode of Nebraskanomics as we welcome Jonathan Williams, co-author of Rich States, Poor States and President and Chief Economist at the American Legislative Exchange Council. During the episode Jonathan and Platte CEO Jim Vokal discuss the latest rankings in new edition of Rich States, Poor States, how Nebraska can improve,...

The post Nebraskanomics: ALEC President Jonathan Williams on Nebraska’s Latest Ranking appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] Taxpayer Rights Amendment Advances
item.description:
The Iowa House is now advancing a proposed constitutional amendment, which would create a requirement that income tax increases receive a two-thirds vote in both chambers of the legislature. The proposed amendment has already passed the Senate and if it passes the full House this spring, it will appear on the ballot for voter approval during November’s election.

The post Taxpayer Rights Amendment Advances appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Government Growth Limit: Louisiana’s Next Step
item.description:
Louisiana is moving in the right direction. Under Governor Jeff Landry, the state is beginning to deal with years of bad policies that weakened competitiveness, slowed job growth, and made too many families look elsewhere for opportunity.  That kind of turnaround does not happen overnight. Markets respond to incentives, but they also need time to...

The post Government Growth Limit: Louisiana’s Next Step appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF calls out City of Albuquerque for wasteful turf spending in KOAT Channel 7 story
item.description:
A few years ago the City of Albuquerque spent $235,000 for turf (in Rio Rancho) for the Duke City Gladiators. The team folded and a new arena team by a different name now plays at State owned Tingley Coliseum. What happened to the old turf? Let’s just say it has been “repurposed.” As the City […]

The post RGF calls out City of Albuquerque for wasteful turf spending in KOAT Channel 7 story appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] Cascade Flags PPS Removal of CBSE from the Voter‑Approved Bond
item.description:
Urges Bond Accountability Committee to strengthen oversight at tonight’s meeting PORTLAND, Ore. — Cascade...

The post Cascade Flags PPS Removal of CBSE from the Voter‑Approved Bond appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Platteinstitute] Sales Tax Modernization Could Help Nebraska Stay Competitive
item.description:
In an increasingly competitive environment, states are competing every day for workers, entrepreneurs, investment, and new residents. Tax policy plays a major role in those decisions. States with more competitive tax structures are often better positioned to attract business investment, encourage job creation, and retain families looking for opportunity.  For Nebraska, that matters. Neighboring states...

The post Sales Tax Modernization Could Help Nebraska Stay Competitive appeared first on Platte Institute.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF Petitions US Supreme Court to Protect Free Speech
item.description:
Thanks to our friends at the Liberty Justice Center the Rio Grande Foundation is appealing for certiorari (an appeal for our case to be heard) against New Mexico Secretary of State Maggie Toulouse Oliver. The case is called: Rio Grande Foundation v. Oliver. The case is based on the proposition that Americans have a constitutional […]

The post RGF Petitions US Supreme Court to Protect Free Speech appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Will Law] Contractors Sue New Jersey Over Forced Unionization and Race-Based Quotas
item.description:
WILL, alongside the Pacific Legal Foundation (PLF) filed a federal lawsuit against the New Jersey Turnpike Authority (NJTA), Ocean County and Evesham Township on behalf of Earle Asphalt Company, a transportation contractor. The lawsuit challenges unconstitutional mandates that force contractors to meet rigid race and sex-based hiring quotas as well as force them to unionize their workforce.  

The post Contractors Sue New Jersey Over Forced Unionization and Race-Based Quotas appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Yankeeinstitute] Senate Advances Conflicting Housing Bills on Shared Living and Utility Costs
item.description:
The Connecticut Senate has advanced two housing proposals that, taken together, could work at cross purposes.  One bill is designed to make it easier for unrelated adults to live together […]

The post Senate Advances Conflicting Housing Bills on Shared Living and Utility Costs appeared first on Yankee Institute.

item.title: [Mainepolicy] 2026 Legislative Wrap-Up
item.description:
As the 132nd Maine Legislature raced toward adjournment, lawmakers pushed through a wave of major bills in just a matter of days. The final stretch of the session was fast-moving, and decisions were made that will shape Maine’s economy, healthcare system, and tax climate for years to come. Maine Policy Institute closely tracked many of […]

The post 2026 Legislative Wrap-Up appeared first on Maine Policy Institute.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] The forgotten subject: California’s failure to assess history education
item.description:
Why students’ history proficiency goes unmeasured, and how a politicized framework is undermining classroom instruction. California has no reliable way to measure whether students are meeting state history and social studies standards. In Gavin Newsom’s State of the State speech in January 2026, he claimed that California has seen “improved academic achievement in every subject...

The post The forgotten subject: California’s failure to assess history education first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Riograndefoundation] RGF in Las Cruces Sun News: Las Cruces overspent. It’s time to cut | Opinion
item.description:
The following appeared in the Las Cruces Sun-News on April 26, 2026. After years of overspending the City of Las Cruces, like Albuquerque, faces a budget deficit.  Unlike Albuquerque which has lost people, Las Cruces continues to grow and has seen a 7.08% increase since the 2020 census. That means that the City can and should be able to […]

The post RGF in Las Cruces Sun News: Las Cruces overspent. It’s time to cut | Opinion appeared first on Rio Grande Foundation.

item.title: [Goldwaterinstitute] What’s Really Behind Arizona Attorney General Mayes’ RealPage Lawsuit?
item.description:
More than two years after Arizona Attorney General Kris Mayes launched a high-profile lawsuit against RealPage, a software company that..

The post What’s Really Behind Arizona Attorney General Mayes’ RealPage Lawsuit? appeared first on Goldwater Institute.

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Milwaukee Public Schools, facing crises, should close 25 schools, report warns
item.description:
Milwaukee Public Schools, amid a fiscal crisis, should close 25 underutilized school buildings to free up wasted resources, says City Forward Collective.

The post Milwaukee Public Schools, facing crises, should close 25 schools, report warns appeared first on Badger Institute.

item.title: [Jbartlett] NH’s low class sizes continue to shrink as enrollment falls faster than teacher rolls
item.description:
New Hampshire public school enrollment continues to decline at a faster rate than teacher employment, a Josiah Bartlett Center for Public Policy analysis of state data shows. Over the last 15 years, public schools in New Hampshire lost students at nearly three times the rate at which they lost teachers. From the 2011-2012 school year […]

The post NH’s low class sizes continue to shrink as enrollment falls faster than teacher rolls appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

Wausau Wins

item.title: [Will Law] Earle Asphalt Company, Inc. v. New Jersey Turnpike Authority
item.description:
WILL alongside the Pacific Legal Foundation (PLF) filed a federal lawsuit against the New Jersey Turnpike Authority (NJTA), Ocean County and Evesham Township on behalf of Earle Asphalt Company, a transportation contractor. The lawsuit challenges unconstitutional mandates that force contractors to meet rigid race and sex-based hiring quotas as well as force them to unionize.

The post Earle Asphalt Company, Inc. v. New Jersey Turnpike Authority appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.title: [Idahofreedom] Saving Money Should Not Be a Thankless Task for Legislators
item.description: Not too long ago, I had a conversation with a staff member of one of Idaho’s US Senators. The conversation centered around why it was so difficult to get federal spending under control - which clearly has not happened. This staffer remarked to me that when Congress cuts spending, they hear loudly from those who […]

item.title: [Badgerinstitute] Socialists’ Milwaukee golden age and the light it sheds now
item.description:
The comparison to Milwaukee’s past Socialist mayors highlights how proposals by current Democratic Socialists are much more radical, even if they’re rhetorically similar.

item.description:
Off-cycle elections are not an accident of scheduling—local governments routinely choose to put major spending decisions up for a vote..

item.title: [Inpolicy] The Outstater
item.description: Unserving ‘the Unhoused’ OUR MAYOR has thrown down the NIMBY gauntlet. She is trying to shame opponents of her plan to build a $3.8 million “homeless services” center downtown, a couple of blocks from the  art museum. She says they selfishly don’t want the center in their backyard but cannot offer an alternative plan. I am Read the full article...

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Ignore the Chicken Littles on Amendment 3: Constitutional Fund Dedications are No Way to Craft a Budget
item.description:
Louisiana voters will consider five constitutional amendments when they got to the polls on May 16. Amendment 3, which makes permanent a teacher pay raise, is probably the most discussed—and the least understood. From heated Facebook posts to mail and texting campaigns, hyperbole about “raiding protected funds” to provide the raises has become a distraction. ...

The post Ignore the Chicken Littles on Amendment 3: Constitutional Fund Dedications are No Way to Craft a Budget appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] AB 1383 Would Gut PEPRA and Worsen California’s Pension Crisis
item.description:
A bill quietly moving through the Legislature would gut California’s 2013 pension reforms and plunge the state back toward the kind of fiscal crisis that forced lawmakers to enact the Public Employees’ Pension Reform Act (PEPRA) in an effort to stabilize a system that was spiraling out of control. AB 1383 (McKinnor) would expand the...

The post AB 1383 Would Gut PEPRA and Worsen California’s Pension Crisis first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Cascadepolicy] The $60 Million Question: Where Did the CBSE Go?
item.description:
A vanished project, a new name, and $60 million in limbo raise serious...

The post The $60 Million Question: Where Did the CBSE Go? appeared first on Cascade Policy Institute.

item.title: [Pelicanpolicy] Sunlight Over Secrecy: Why HB 763 Matters
item.description:
Louisiana works best when government operates in the open. At the Pelican Institute, that’s a principle we return to again and again because it’s foundational to getting policy right and maintaining trust in government. House Bill 763 by Representative Beth Billings is a strong step in that direction, and it’s encouraging to see the House...

The post Sunlight Over Secrecy: Why HB 763 Matters appeared first on Pelican Policy.

item.title: [Mspolicy] Questions Mississippi Lawmakers Should Ask Regarding Medicaid
item.description:
Mississippi lawmakers say they’ll spend part of the summer studying the state’s Medicaid program to see if there’s a way to save taxpayer money. But if recent history is any indication, the effort will not yield significant fruit. Lawmakers in other states have, for years, picked through the federal-state medical welfare partnership, and so far, […]

The post Questions Mississippi Lawmakers Should Ask Regarding Medicaid appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] Is college still worth the cost in the Mountain West?
item.description: In states where every dollar and opportunity matters, such as Wyoming and Montana, improving transparency could be just as important as expanding access, ensuring that students who attend college truly benefit from it.

item.title: [Mspolicy] From Classroom to Culture: Teaching the Ideas That Make Societies Prosper
item.description:
While others debate these ideas from a distance, we’re in the room teaching them. This semester, Douglas Carswell was invited to serve as a guest lecturer at the Declaration Center for Freedom Studies at the University of Mississippi—spending the semester working directly with students on the principles that make societies prosper. Week after week, he […]

The post From Classroom to Culture: Teaching the Ideas That Make Societies Prosper appeared first on Mississippi Center for Public Policy.

item.title: [Mountainstatespolicy] Who’s getting the Idaho parental choice tax credit - and why is the application window reopening?
item.description: Early data obtained by MSPC shows that nearly half of participating families are lower-income or working class. That matters. And it tells u

item.title: [Itrfoundation] New Law Puts Limits on Property Tax Levies in Iowa
item.description:
Iowa lawmakers passed a comprehensive property tax reform bill featuring a 2% cap on city and county property tax growth, marking a meaningful step toward slowing long-term tax increases. While the cap is significant, some exemptions and uncapped levies remain, meaning continued vigilance is needed as some local governments may seek to work around the limits. The bill includes several additional reforms, including reducing the school levy rate, restructuring homestead benefits, limiting TIF durations, capping ending fund balances, and improving taxpayer transparency through clearer notices and data access.

The post New Law Puts Limits on Property Tax Levies in Iowa appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Pioneerinstitute] Boston is 48th out of 55 Metro Areas in the Competition for Young Workers
item.description:
A new analysis from ADP Research (reported in the Wall Street Journal) delivers a clear warning for Massachusetts: the Boston–Cambridge–Newton metropolitan area now ranks 48th out of 55 major U.S. metro areas in attracting young workers. The study tracks more than 400,000 workers in their 20s and evaluates metro areas on a simple but decisive set of factors—job availability, affordability, and access to degree-requiring work. On those measures, Boston lands near the bottom. High wages are no longer enough to offset the region’s high cost structure and weaker hiring momentum.

The post Boston is 48th out of 55 Metro Areas in the Competition for Young Workers appeared first on Pioneer Institute.

item.title: [Mackinac] States can cure federal pharma fever
item.description: D.C. bipartisanship is making the patient sicker

More than 160 districts in Illinois bar participation by those who aren’t full-time students in their schools.

item.title: [Will Law] WILL Unveils New Documentary: Classrooms in Crisis
item.description:
In honor of Teacher Appreciation Week, WILL unveiled a new documentary, “Classrooms in Crisis,” putting human faces on the growing crisis of student behavior, school safety, and teacher authority in Wisconsin classrooms. Through the firsthand accounts of real educators, many of whom left public schools out of fear for their own safety, the mini-documentary reveals an education system under serious strain.

The post WILL Unveils New Documentary: Classrooms in Crisis appeared first on Wisconsin Institute for Law & Liberty.

item.description: It is Gov. Ferguson’s decision whether Washington families will benefit, or if the money will instead flow to students in participating states.

As part of a broader trip to tour the State Capitol and meet with legislators, the group made time to sit down with Douglas Carswell and our team to learn […]

item.description:

Tan Parker used a recent appearance on The Arena with Greg Sindelar to argue that Texas is entering a decisive period—one in which cyber defense, artificial intelligence, financial markets, transportation and traditional family values will determine whether the state continues to lead the country. Parker framed public service as “ministry” and said his...

item.title: [Texaspolicy] There is More Than One Way to SAVE America
item.description:
If Congress cannot quickly pass the SAVE America Act, policymakers should focus on what can be done now to advance its core provisions. As debate over election integrity continues in Washington, President Donald Trump has kept the SAVE America Act at the forefront, urging action for its passage in the Senate. At the same time, Democratic lawmakers...

What began as a targeted effort to limit noncompete agreements for lower-wage workers is quietly evolving into something considerably broader.  Noncompete agreements — contracts that restrict where employees can work […]

item.title: [Kansaspolicy] Fuel Prices Need Abundance
item.description: Kansas families need lower fuel costs through more supply, less inflation, and fewer government-made costs. Fuel prices hit families fast. A gallon of gas is not just a number on a sign. It is the cost of getting to work, taking kids to school, delivering goods, that long-planned summer road trip, and keeping rural Kansas […]

item.title: [Pacificresearch] San Francisco’s Justice System Is Breaking Down
item.description:
San Francisco’s criminal justice system may be reaching a breaking point. A San Francisco Superior Court judge recently found Public Defender Mano Raju in contempt and imposed fines after the office continued declining court-assigned cases in defiance of a prior order to stop doing so. District Attorney Brooke Jenkins says the refusals are disrupting court […]

The post San Francisco’s Justice System Is Breaking Down appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Beacontn] Monumental Wins, Unfinished Business: Tennessee’s 2026 Legislative Session and the Road Ahead
item.description: On Thursday, April 23, 2026, the 114th Tennessee General Assembly adjourned sine die. From efforts to regulate data centers and pharmacy benefit managers, to a framework for state takeover of Tennessee’s lowest-performing school districts, legislators saw their fair share of robust policy debates this year.

item.title: [Alabamapolicy] The Business of Hate: Why Alabama and the Nation Must Reject the SPLC’s Extremism
item.description:

item.title: [Johnlocke] Philadelphia museum focuses on America’s faults as 250th anniversary approaches
item.description:
Ira Stoll writes for the Washington Free Beacon about one museum’s questionable approach to the nation’s 250th anniversary. The Philadelphia Museum of Art—in the city where the Declaration of Independence was drafted and signed—is marking the 250th anniversary of the founding of America with an exhibit that harps on our country’s flaws so obsessively that...

The post Philadelphia museum focuses on America’s faults as 250th anniversary approaches appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.title: [Johnlocke] Alito counters colleague’s unhinged dissent
item.description:
Shawn Fleetwood of the Federalist highlights US Supreme Court Justice Samuel Alito’s response to a colleague’s misguided writing. Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson’s Supreme Court opinions are often poorly argued and lack coherent legal reasoning. And in the high court’s latest release, Justice Samuel Alito made sure she knew it. The Alito-driven smackdown happened late Monday...

The post Alito counters colleague’s unhinged dissent appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.description:
Victoria Churchill writes for the Daily Mail about former President Barack Obama’s latest complaint. Barack Obama has offered a rare and candid glimpse into his marriage – and it turns out Donald Trump is causing trouble at home. The former President, 64, admitted in an interview that Michelle wants him to step back from public...

The post Obama claims Trump has spoiled his marital bliss appeared first on John Locke Foundation.

item.description: If activists cared about children, they would reach across the aisle

item.title: [Pacificresearch] Beyond trailer parks: Modular housing can boost affordability
item.description:
The nation enjoys a “very high rate of economic activity,” yet “housing construction hasn’t been high.” Inflation’s a major factor — “land is several times more expensive than it’s been in the past,” and costs for “materials have gone way up.” Paradoxically, “we’ve been losing ground in meeting housing needs in this country, despite all […]

The post Beyond trailer parks: Modular housing can boost affordability appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.

item.title: [Illinoispolicy] Under Pritzker, Illinois near the bottom in job growth
item.description:
The state created only 54,000 jobs in seven years.

The post Under Pritzker, Illinois near the bottom in job growth appeared first on Illinois Policy.

item.title: [Itrfoundation] Legislature Passes Transparency for Federal Funding
item.description:
Federal funds aren’t “free”—they come with strings attached, can drive higher state spending, and are increasingly unstable given Washington’s fiscal position and growing national debt. Earlier in the session, HSB 764 proposed a stronger approach with upfront reporting, full transparency, and legislative approval of major federal funds, ensuring lawmakers could evaluate commitments before they were made. Lawmakers ultimately passed HF 2800, which improves transparency by requiring notification of large or state-matched federal funds—an important step forward, but one that stops short of full oversight and approval.

The post Legislature Passes Transparency for Federal Funding appeared first on ITR Foundation.

item.title: [Jbartlett] Two labor law updates for the 21st century
item.description:
Remote work, flex time and independent contracting have reshaped the American workforce, creating new opportunities for both employers and employees. But in some states, including New Hampshire, outdated labor laws create obstacles to innovation, lost opportunities for employees and unnecessary costs for employers.  To modernize New Hampshire labor laws, legislators are addressing two outdated statutory […]

The post Two labor law updates for the 21st century appeared first on THE JOSIAH BARTLETT CENTER FOR PUBLIC POLICY.

item.title: [Texaspolicy] UT Austin’s New Civic Leadership School Takes Aim at “Anti-Civic Education”
item.description:
A recent episode of Parent Empowerment with Mandy Drogin offered a pointed look at the University of Texas at Austin’s new School of Civic Leadership and the broader fight over what American students should be taught about Western civilization, religion, economics, and the nation’s founding ideals. Guest Justin Dyer, dean of the school, outlined the...

The post UT Austin’s New Civic Leadership School Takes Aim at “Anti-Civic Education” first appeared on Texas Public Policy Foundation.

item.title: [Californiapolicycenter] Chino Valley Unified Moves to Dissolve Court Injunction Following SCOTUS Mirabelli Ruling on Parental Rights
item.description:
SAN BERNARDINO, CA — The Chino Valley Unified School District (CVUSD) has filed a motion to dissolve a 2024 court-ordered injunction that blocked the district’s parental notification policy. Following the U.S. Supreme Court’s recent decision in Mirabelli v. Bonta, Chino Valley is going back to court to remove the original injunction against it. In March,...

The post Chino Valley Unified Moves to Dissolve Court Injunction Following SCOTUS Mirabelli Ruling on Parental Rights first appeared on California Policy Center.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] DFL’s new tax on social media is still a bad idea
item.description:
Last year, the Tax Committee in the Minnesota Senate, where the DFL holds a one seat majority, passed a bill that would have imposed the first “social media tax” in the nation. It didn’t go anywhere with Republicans holding half of the seats in the House, but no bad idea ever truly dies, and it...

The post DFL’s new tax on social media is still a bad idea appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] A growing divide in how teachers view student learning progress
item.description:
National Teacher Appreciation Week is a time to recognize the commitment and impact of educators across the country. But beyond the celebratory headlines (guilty!), new survey data points to a more complicated picture inside America’s classrooms. Teachers in public schools and private schools are reporting very different levels of confidence in whether their students are...

The post A growing divide in how teachers view student learning progress appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Americanexperiment] The school district using easy levers to encourage students to sleep more
item.description:
Even though high schoolers need at least 8 hours of sleep per night, over three in four high schoolers are classified as having insufficient sleep schedules. Unsurprisingly, students who sleep less than they should tend to struggle with attention and cognitive retention for the rest of the day. Spokane’s school district sought to change that...

The post The school district using easy levers to encourage students to sleep more appeared first on American Experiment.

item.title: [Pacificresearch] A $20,000 model trashcan showcases San Francisco’s dysfunction
item.description:
Three years ago, my East Coast relatives flew to San Francisco for my daughter’s wedding. At the time, national publications were having a field day depicting the city as a pit of decay filled with poop-covered sidewalks and rampant homelessness. My relatives were primed to see an urban landscape beset by used needles and graffiti-covered […]

The post A $20,000 model trashcan showcases San Francisco’s dysfunction appeared first on Pacific Research Institute.
